steps.title: Energy stored in a squish
steps.body:
Pull back a bow, press down a trampoline, pull a pinball plunger — you're loading a spring with energy that springs right back.

A compressed or stretched spring stores **elastic potential energy**. Squeeze it more and it stores a lot more.

steps.title: The formula
steps.body:
**k** is the spring constant — how stiff it is (N/m). **x** is how far it's pushed or pulled from its natural length (m).

Like kinetic energy, **x is squared** — stretch counts double.

steps.title: Get a feel for it
steps.body: Squeeze the spring and change its stiffness. Notice how squeeze (x) swings the energy far more than stiffness — that's the x² at work.

steps.prompt: You compress a spring twice as far. What happens to the energy it stores?
hints: U_s = ½kx² — the stretch is squared.
Double x → x² grows by 4. So the energy quadruples.

steps.prompt: Plug in the numbers. A spring has **k = 200 N/m** and is squeezed **x = 0.3 m**. Drag each value into its spot, then find U_s.
steps.fillHint: Stiffness goes in the k spot, squeeze in the x spot. Here k = 200 and x = 0.3.

steps.title: Let it go
steps.body: Release the spring and that stored energy doesn't vanish — it pushes the block into motion. With no friction, **all the elastic PE becomes kinetic energy**.

steps.prompt: The spring (**k = 200 N/m**, compressed **0.4 m**) launches a block — no friction. Drag the **ΔKE** the block gains.
hints: Spring PE converts fully to KE (no losses).
ΔKE = U_s = 16 J from the 0.4 m compression.

steps.title: Lesson complete!
steps.body: You sized spring energy and watched it transfer to kinetic energy.
